\appendix[AI-Based Development Approach]
\label{sec:ai-development}

The implementation described in this paper was developed in close collaboration with AI coding assistants --- primarily Claude Code, with Codex consulted as a second opinion on critical design decisions.
The approach relied on a self-validating feedback loop: verification and specification inference (Sec.~\ref{sec:validation}) were developed in parallel, so that any change to the encoding immediately had a baseline of test cases against which the AI could check itself --- the inferred specs must verify end-to-end, with no human in the loop required to assess correctness.

\Paragraph{What went well}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Specification language extensions in the frontend.}  Parser and type-checker changes for behavioral predicates and state labels were largely produced by the AI, with human effort limited to review.
    \item \emph{Bug identification and fixing.}  Once a problematic example was unearthed --- typically by inference producing a spec that failed verification --- the AI was effective at diagnosing the root cause and proposing a principled fix.
    \item AI was very code at diagnosing verification problems like timeouts, by analyzing generated Boogie and SMT code, using Z3's logging features to observe quantifier instantiation, etc.
    \item \emph{Boilerplate and well-understood areas.}  Routine code such as expression simplification, AST traversals, and bytecode-level rewrites was handled essentially autonomously.
\end{itemize}

\Paragraph{What went less well}
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Hand-holding in the complex parts.}  The Boogie encoding of behavioral predicates, the discrimination over closure variants, and the existential treatment of intermediate state labels all required substantial human guidance.
    The AI's first attempts were rarely correct for the general case, and judgement calls about soundness needed to be made by the human.
    \item \emph{Many iterations.}  Reaching a correct design often required dozens of iterations and significant elapsed time; in the most complex areas, the total effort approached that of manual implementation.
    \item \emph{Token cost.}  Long-running iterative work over a large codebase accumulates substantial inference cost.
\end{itemize}

Overall, the experience was positive.
The validation pipeline gave the AI an unambiguous correctness signal independent of human review, and the AI carried a disproportionate share of the work in areas where the design space was already well understood.
The harder, design-novel parts remained human-driven --- but the AI made the surrounding work substantially less laborious.
